topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The vase sat on the reclaimed wood table, a garish display of synthetic pinks and wilting yellows. I picked up a stem, the petals feeling strangely slick under my fingers. There was no note, just the clumsy arrangement of blooms, a silent, unsolicited declaration. I stared at the arrangement, my jaw tightening almost imperceptibly. It felt like a performance, an unnecessary flourish designed to elicit some kind of response. I turned the bouquet over, examining the cheap cellophane wrapping, before letting it drop back onto the wood with a soft, dismissive thud. I didn't bother to look at the sender, focusing instead on the dust motes dancing in the afternoon sunbeam slicing across the room.

[story 2]

When the delivery arrived, it was a startling interruption. A bouquet of lilies, impossibly perfect and aggressively fragrant, left on the doorstep. I paused at the threshold, my expression hardening into something unreadable. I didn't move to take them in. My gaze swept over the flowers, cataloging their obvious effort, the sheer volume of them feeling excessive. "What is this?" I managed, my voice clipped and devoid of warmth. The person who had sent them hovered awkwardly, clearly expecting some reaction, some gratitude. I simply shook my head, a slow, deliberate motion that communicated a profound lack of interest, and retreated inside, leaving the vibrant, silent offering behind.

[story 3]

I found the flowers tucked into the corner of the hallway, a vibrant splash against the muted beige paint. They were bright, too bright, demanding attention. I walked over slowly, my steps measured and heavy, as if navigating a minefield. I didn't touch them immediately. My eyes narrowed as I assessed the arrangement, noting the slightly askew placement of the stems—a sign of hurriedness, perhaps. A faint, almost imperceptible tightening around my eyes suggested deep disapproval. "Seriously?" I murmured, the sound flat, laced with a dry edge. I didn't wait for an explanation; the gesture itself felt misplaced, a clumsy attempt to bridge a gap that clearly wasn't there. I simply stepped around the display, moving toward the kitchen as if the flowers were an irritating piece of furniture.
